message:
raptorq: add dense-factor reuse cache and broaden decode stress benchmarks

Context:
RaptorQ decode hot paths repeatedly rebuild dense elimination scaffolding for
similar equation structures. This commit adds deterministic cache-backed reuse
and extends benchmark/test surfaces to guard performance-sensitive scenarios.

What changed:
- `src/raptorq/decoder.rs`
  - Extended `DecodeStats` with dense-factor cache telemetry:
    hits/misses/inserts/evictions, fingerprint-collision counts, last-key,
    decision reason, reuse eligibility, entry count, and cache capacity.
  - Added deterministic dense-factor signature + artifact model:
    `DenseFactorSignature`, `DenseFactorArtifact`, and FIFO-bounded
    `DenseFactorCache` (capacity = 16).
  - Added `dense_factor_with_cache(...)` to probe/reuse/rebuild dense skeleton
    metadata based on strict signature match, including explicit fingerprint
    collision handling.
  - Wired decode paths to use cache observations and update stats via
    `apply_dense_factor_cache_observation(...)`.
  - Added focused unit tests for:
    signature sensitivity, strict cache match behavior, fingerprint-collision
    detection, bounded eviction policy, and second-run cache-hit behavior on
    repeated repair-only decode bursts.

- `benches/raptorq_benchmark.rs`
  - Introduced shared helper `build_decode_received(...)` to construct decode
    input sets for source/repair mixes.
  - Added benchmark surfaces for heavier failure modes:
    `decode_repair_heavy` (75% source loss + repair margin) and
    `decode_near_rank_deficient` (clustered 50% source loss, minimal overhead).
  - Simplified existing decode benchmark setup to use shared construction logic.

- `tests/raptorq_perf_invariants.rs`
  - Added F4 guardrail asserting required benchmark surfaces/tokens remain in
    `benches/raptorq_benchmark.rs`.

Why this matters:
- Repeated structural decode patterns can now reuse dense-factor metadata
  deterministically instead of rebuilding every time.
- Telemetry makes cache behavior explicit and testable.
- Benchmark coverage now includes more adversarial decode distributions,
  reducing risk of regressions hidden by source-dominant workloads.
